planMarkdown:
Bot: Indonesia–English Auto-Translate Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that automatically translates every incoming user message between Indonesian and English, reversing the translation direction based on detected input language. The bot works in private chats and can optionally support group chats with configurable behavior.

The plan focuses on a single-process Node.js service using grammY, MongoDB for persistence, and a modular architecture that supports rate limiting, abuse prevention, observability, and safe fallbacks when language detection is uncertain. The bot is designed to be simple for users (send text, get translation) while still being reliable and maintainable in production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Provide instant bidirectional translation between Indonesian and English. If a user sends Indonesian, the bot replies in English; if a user sends English, the bot replies in Indonesian.

User experience goals:
1) Zero-configuration default: Users can start translating immediately after /start.
2) Predictable replies: The bot responds to every supported message with a single translated response.
3) Fast and reliable: Low latency, with sensible fallbacks if detection or translation fails.
4) Clear behavior in edge cases: If language detection is ambiguous or text contains mixed languages, the bot explains what it did and offers a way to override.

Scope decisions:
1) Translate text content. Optionally include captions on photos/documents if present.
2) Do not attempt full OCR or speech-to-text as a baseline requirement.
3) Default translation direction is automatic, but users can lock direction (ID->EN or EN->ID) per chat.

Success criteria:
1) High accuracy for typical conversational text.
2) Minimal operational burden: stable single service, straightforward monitoring, and controlled costs.
3) Safe handling of spam and large messages while staying within Telegram limits.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) using grammY. The bot receives updates via either webhook or long polling. For production readiness, webhook is preferred if the environment supports inbound HTTP, otherwise long polling remains acceptable with careful error handling.

Core modules (separation of concerns):
1) Update handling layer: grammY bot instance, middleware chain, command router, message handlers.
2) Session and settings module: per-chat settings (auto vs locked direction, group behavior, verbosity), with MongoDB persistence.
3) Language detection module: lightweight detection tuned for Indonesian vs English, using a deterministic heuristic first and an external detector second (optional) to reduce cost/latency. Must return confidence score and a reason.
4) Translation provider module: abstraction over a translation API/service with retries, timeouts, circuit breaker behavior, and usage accounting.
5) Policy and safety module: rate limiting per user/chat, message size limits, content filtering for unsupported inputs, and group privacy controls.
6) Observability module: structured logging, metrics counters (translations, errors, latency), and correlation IDs per update.

Request lifecycle:
1) Receive update.
2) Identify chat/user, load settings and minimal user profile.
3) If command: execute command.
4) If text/caption: run policy checks, detect language, decide direction, translate, respond.
5) Persist audit record (optional) and update usage counters.

Error handling principles:
1) Fail fast for unsupported message types with a short guidance reply.
2) For transient translation failures: retry once with backoff; if still failing, respond with a human-readable error.
3) For language detection ambiguity: ask the user to choose direction or provide a one-tap setting command.

Group chat behavior (recommended defaults):
1) Only translate when the bot is directly replied to, or when a message starts with a trigger (for example, “tr:” or “translate ”). This prevents spam in groups.
2) Allow admins to enable always-on translation per group via a setting command.

Deployment shape:
1) One Node.js server process.
2) MongoDB for persistence.
3) Optional object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later add features like saving translation history exports or logs; not required for baseline translator.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal, production-friendly):

1) chats
Fields:
1) chatId (number/string)
2) type (private/group/supergroup)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) settings:
   1) mode: auto | locked
   2) lockedDirection: idtoen | entoid (only if mode=locked)
   3) groupTrigger: replyonly | prefixonly | always
   4) showDetectedLanguage: boolean
   5) showConfidenceWarnings: boolean
   6) maxCharsPerMessage: number

2) users
Fields:
1) userId
2) firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt
3) preferredMode (optional override for private chats)
4) usage:
   1) dayKey (YYYY-MM-DD)
   2) translatedCharsToday
   3) requestsToday

3) translationlogs (optional, can be disabled for privacy)
Purpose: debugging and quality improvements, not required for core functionality.
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) chatId, userId
3) inputLength
4) detectedSourceLang (id/en/unknown)
5) targetLang
6) confidence
7) providerLatencyMs
8) status (success/error)
9) errorCode (if any)

Privacy-first approach:
1) Do not store raw message text by default.
2) If logs are enabled, store only minimal metadata unless the user/admin explicitly opts in to diagnostics.
3) Implement retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.

Indexes:
1) chats.chatId unique
2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces the bot and starts auto-translation in the current chat. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows usage instructions, examples, and common troubleshooting. (usage: /help) [core]
• /mode — Sets translation mode: auto (detect) or locked (fixed direction). (usage: /mode auto | /mode locked) [settings]
• /id2en — Locks translation direction to Indonesian -> English for this chat. (usage: /id2en) [settings]
• /en2id — Locks translation direction to English -> Indonesian for this chat. (usage: /en2id) [settings]
• /auto — Shortcut to return to automatic language detection mode. (usage: /auto) [settings]
• /settings — Displays current chat settings and how to change them. (usage: /settings) [settings]
• /groupmode — Configures how the bot behaves in groups (admins only). (usage: /groupmode replyonly | /groupmode prefixonly | /groupmode always) [admin]
• /privacy — Explains what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /delete — Deletes stored user data for the requesting user (and private chat settings). (usage: /delete) [trust]
• /optoutlogs — Disables optional diagnostics logging for the current chat/user. (usage: /optoutlogs) [trust]
• /status — Shows basic service status and current rate limit counters for the user (optional). (usage: /status) [ops]

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot automatically translates messages between Indonesian (id) and English (en).

Default behavior
In a new chat, auto-translate is ON and direction is AUTO.
That means:
1) Indonesian input is translated to English.
2) English input is translated to Indonesian.
3) If detection is uncertain, it defaults to translating into English.

Private vs group chats
1) Private chats: the bot replies to every non-command text message (unless auto-translate is turned off).
2) Group chats: the bot replies only when you reply to a bot message OR mention the bot.

Commands
/start
What it does: Welcome message and quick examples.
Usage: /start

/help
What it does: Detailed usage and limitations.
Usage: /help

/auto_on
What it does: Enables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_on

/auto_off
What it does: Disables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_off

/toen
What it does: Forces translation target to English for the current chat.
Usage: /toen

/toid
What it does: Forces translation target to Indonesian for the current chat.
Usage: /toid

/mode
What it does: Shows current settings for the chat.
Usage: /mode

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram bot token.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
Required. Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway.
Example format: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY
Required. API key for CookMyBots AI Gateway.

MONGODB_URI
Optional but recommended. MongoDB connection string used to persist per-chat settings.

LOG_LEVEL
Optional. Default is info.

Running locally
1) npm install
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and fill values
3) npm run dev

README.md.content:
This is a Telegram translation bot built with grammY (Node.js ES modules).

It automatically translates between Indonesian (id) and English (en).
If you send Indonesian, it replies in English. If you send English, it replies in Indonesian.

In group chats, the bot replies only when:
1) You reply to a bot message, or
2) You mention the bot (for example @YourBotUsername)

Setup
1) Install deps
npm install

2) Create a .env file (or set Render env vars) based on .env.sample

Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY

Optional but recommended
1) MONGODB_URI (persists per-chat settings across restarts)

Run locally
npm run dev

Run in production
npm start

Commands
/start
Shows a welcome message and quick examples.

/help
Shows detailed help.

/auto_on
Enable auto-translate for the current chat.

/auto_off
Disable auto-translate for the current chat.

/toen
Force translations to English for this chat.

/toid
Force translations to Indonesian for this chat.

/mode
Show current chat settings.

Notes
1) Only text messages are translated.
2) If MONGODB_URI is missing, the bot still works but settings reset on restart.
3) The bot uses CookMyBots AI Gateway only (no direct OpenAI calls).

  const name = botUsername ? ("@" + botUsername) : "this bot";

    "Bot purpose: Automatically translate chat messages between Indonesian (id) and English (en).",
    "Behavior: For normal text messages, detect if input is Indonesian or English, then translate to the opposite language unless a forced direction is set.",
    "Public commands:",
    "/start - Welcome and quick examples",
    "/help - Detailed usage",
    "/auto_on - Enable auto-translate for this chat",
    "/auto_off - Disable auto-translate for this chat",
    "/toen - Force translating into English",
    "/toid - Force translating into Indonesian",
    "/mode - Show current settings",
    "Key rules:",
    "1) Only translate non-command text (messages starting with / are commands and must not be translated).",
    "2) In group chats, respond only when mentioned by " + name + " or when the message is a reply to a bot message.",
    "3) Reply should be the translated text only (no extra commentary)."
  ].join("\n");
}

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Indonesia–English Auto-Translate Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that automatically translates every incoming user message between Indonesian and English, reversing the translation direction based on detected input language. The bot works in private chats and can optionally support group chats with configurable behavior.

The plan focuses on a single-process Node.js service using grammY, MongoDB for persistence, and a modular architecture that supports rate limiting, abuse prevention, observability, and safe fallbacks when language detection is uncertain. The bot is designed to be simple for users (send text, get translation) while still being reliable and maintainable in production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Provide instant bidirectional translation between Indonesian and English. If a user sends Indonesian, the bot replies in English; if a user sends English, the bot replies in Indonesian.

User experience goals:
1) Zero-configuration default: Users can start translating immediately after /start.
2) Predictable replies: The bot responds to every supported message with a single translated response.
3) Fast and reliable: Low latency, with sensible fallbacks if detection or translation fails.
4) Clear behavior in edge cases: If language detection is ambiguous or text contains mixed languages, the bot explains what it did and offers a way to override.

Scope decisions:
1) Translate text content. Optionally include captions on photos/documents if present.
2) Do not attempt full OCR or speech-to-text as a baseline requirement.
3) Default translation direction is automatic, but users can lock direction (ID->EN or EN->ID) per chat.

Success criteria:
1) High accuracy for typical conversational text.
2) Minimal operational burden: stable single service, straightforward monitoring, and controlled costs.
3) Safe handling of spam and large messages while staying within Telegram limits.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) using grammY. The bot receives updates via either webhook or long polling. For production readiness, webhook is preferred if the environment supports inbound HTTP, otherwise long polling remains acceptable with careful error handling.

Core modules (separation of concerns):
1) Update handling layer: grammY bot instance, middleware chain, command router, message handlers.
2) Session and settings module: per-chat settings (auto vs locked direction, group behavior, verbosity), with MongoDB persistence.
3) Language detection module: lightweight detection tuned for Indonesian vs English, using a deterministic heuristic first and an external detector second (optional) to reduce cost/latency. Must return confidence score and a reason.
4) Translation provider module: abstraction over a translation API/service with retries, timeouts, circuit breaker behavior, and usage accounting.
5) Policy and safety module: rate limiting per user/chat, message size limits, content filtering for unsupported inputs, and group privacy controls.
6) Observability module: structured logging, metrics counters (translations, errors, latency), and correlation IDs per update.

Request lifecycle:
1) Receive update.
2) Identify chat/user, load settings and minimal user profile.
3) If command: execute command.
4) If text/caption: run policy checks, detect language, decide direction, translate, respond.
5) Persist audit record (optional) and update usage counters.

Error handling principles:
1) Fail fast for unsupported message types with a short guidance reply.
2) For transient translation failures: retry once with backoff; if still failing, respond with a human-readable error.
3) For language detection ambiguity: ask the user to choose direction or provide a one-tap setting command.

Group chat behavior (recommended defaults):
1) Only translate when the bot is directly replied to, or when a message starts with a trigger (for example, “tr:” or “translate ”). This prevents spam in groups.
2) Allow admins to enable always-on translation per group via a setting command.

Deployment shape:
1) One Node.js server process.
2) MongoDB for persistence.
3) Optional object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later add features like saving translation history exports or logs; not required for baseline translator.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal, production-friendly):

1) chats
Fields:
1) chatId (number/string)
2) type (private/group/supergroup)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) settings:
   1) mode: auto | locked
   2) lockedDirection: idtoen | entoid (only if mode=locked)
   3) groupTrigger: replyonly | prefixonly | always
   4) showDetectedLanguage: boolean
   5) showConfidenceWarnings: boolean
   6) maxCharsPerMessage: number

2) users
Fields:
1) userId
2) firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt
3) preferredMode (optional override for private chats)
4) usage:
   1) dayKey (YYYY-MM-DD)
   2) translatedCharsToday
   3) requestsToday

3) translationlogs (optional, can be disabled for privacy)
Purpose: debugging and quality improvements, not required for core functionality.
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) chatId, userId
3) inputLength
4) detectedSourceLang (id/en/unknown)
5) targetLang
6) confidence
7) providerLatencyMs
8) status (success/error)
9) errorCode (if any)

Privacy-first approach:
1) Do not store raw message text by default.
2) If logs are enabled, store only minimal metadata unless the user/admin explicitly opts in to diagnostics.
3) Implement retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.

Indexes:
1) chats.chatId unique
2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces the bot and starts auto-translation in the current chat. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows usage instructions, examples, and common troubleshooting. (usage: /help) [core]
• /mode — Sets translation mode: auto (detect) or locked (fixed direction). (usage: /mode auto | /mode locked) [settings]
• /id2en — Locks translation direction to Indonesian -> English for this chat. (usage: /id2en) [settings]
• /en2id — Locks translation direction to English -> Indonesian for this chat. (usage: /en2id) [settings]
• /auto — Shortcut to return to automatic language detection mode. (usage: /auto) [settings]
• /settings — Displays current chat settings and how to change them. (usage: /settings) [settings]
• /groupmode — Configures how the bot behaves in groups (admins only). (usage: /groupmode replyonly | /groupmode prefixonly | /groupmode always) [admin]
• /privacy — Explains what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /delete — Deletes stored user data for the requesting user (and private chat settings). (usage: /delete) [trust]
• /optoutlogs — Disables optional diagnostics logging for the current chat/user. (usage: /optoutlogs) [trust]
• /status — Shows basic service status and current rate limit counters for the user (optional). (usage: /status) [ops]

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot automatically translates messages between Indonesian (id) and English (en).

Default behavior
In a new chat, auto-translate is ON and direction is AUTO.
That means:
1) Indonesian input is translated to English.
2) English input is translated to Indonesian.
3) If detection is uncertain, it defaults to translating into English.

Private vs group chats
1) Private chats: the bot replies to every non-command text message (unless auto-translate is turned off).
2) Group chats: the bot replies only when you reply to a bot message OR mention the bot.

Commands
/start
What it does: Welcome message and quick examples.
Usage: /start

/help
What it does: Detailed usage and limitations.
Usage: /help

/auto_on
What it does: Enables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_on

/auto_off
What it does: Disables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_off

/toen
What it does: Forces translation target to English for the current chat.
Usage: /toen

/toid
What it does: Forces translation target to Indonesian for the current chat.
Usage: /toid

/mode
What it does: Shows current settings for the chat.
Usage: /mode

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram bot token.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
Required. Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway.
Example format: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY
Required. API key for CookMyBots AI Gateway.

MONGODB_URI
Optional but recommended. MongoDB connection string used to persist per-chat settings.

LOG_LEVEL
Optional. Default is info.

Running locally
1) npm install
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and fill values
3) npm run dev

README.md.content:
This is a Telegram translation bot built with grammY (Node.js ES modules).

It automatically translates between Indonesian (id) and English (en).
If you send Indonesian, it replies in English. If you send English, it replies in Indonesian.

In group chats, the bot replies only when:
1) You reply to a bot message, or
2) You mention the bot (for example @YourBotUsername)

Setup
1) Install deps
npm install

2) Create a .env file (or set Render env vars) based on .env.sample

Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY

Optional but recommended
1) MONGODB_URI (persists per-chat settings across restarts)

Run locally
npm run dev

Run in production
npm start

Commands
/start
Shows a welcome message and quick examples.

/help
Shows detailed help.

/auto_on
Enable auto-translate for the current chat.

/auto_off
Disable auto-translate for the current chat.

/toen
Force translations to English for this chat.

/toid
Force translations to Indonesian for this chat.

/mode
Show current chat settings.

Notes
1) Only text messages are translated.
2) If MONGODB_URI is missing, the bot still works but settings reset on restart.
3) The bot uses CookMyBots AI Gateway only (no direct OpenAI calls).

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Indonesia–English Auto-Translate Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that automatically translates every incoming user message between Indonesian and English, reversing the translation direction based on detected input language. The bot works in private chats and can optionally support group chats with configurable behavior.

The plan focuses on a single-process Node.js service using grammY, MongoDB for persistence, and a modular architecture that supports rate limiting, abuse prevention, observability, and safe fallbacks when language detection is uncertain. The bot is designed to be simple for users (send text, get translation) while still being reliable and maintainable in production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Provide instant bidirectional translation between Indonesian and English. If a user sends Indonesian, the bot replies in English; if a user sends English, the bot replies in Indonesian.

User experience goals:
1) Zero-configuration default: Users can start translating immediately after /start.
2) Predictable replies: The bot responds to every supported message with a single translated response.
3) Fast and reliable: Low latency, with sensible fallbacks if detection or translation fails.
4) Clear behavior in edge cases: If language detection is ambiguous or text contains mixed languages, the bot explains what it did and offers a way to override.

Scope decisions:
1) Translate text content. Optionally include captions on photos/documents if present.
2) Do not attempt full OCR or speech-to-text as a baseline requirement.
3) Default translation direction is automatic, but users can lock direction (ID->EN or EN->ID) per chat.

Success criteria:
1) High accuracy for typical conversational text.
2) Minimal operational burden: stable single service, straightforward monitoring, and controlled costs.
3) Safe handling of spam and large messages while staying within Telegram limits.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) using grammY. The bot receives updates via either webhook or long polling. For production readiness, webhook is preferred if the environment supports inbound HTTP, otherwise long polling remains acceptable with careful error handling.

Core modules (separation of concerns):
1) Update handling layer: grammY bot instance, middleware chain, command router, message handlers.
2) Session and settings module: per-chat settings (auto vs locked direction, group behavior, verbosity), with MongoDB persistence.
3) Language detection module: lightweight detection tuned for Indonesian vs English, using a deterministic heuristic first and an external detector second (optional) to reduce cost/latency. Must return confidence score and a reason.
4) Translation provider module: abstraction over a translation API/service with retries, timeouts, circuit breaker behavior, and usage accounting.
5) Policy and safety module: rate limiting per user/chat, message size limits, content filtering for unsupported inputs, and group privacy controls.
6) Observability module: structured logging, metrics counters (translations, errors, latency), and correlation IDs per update.

Request lifecycle:
1) Receive update.
2) Identify chat/user, load settings and minimal user profile.
3) If command: execute command.
4) If text/caption: run policy checks, detect language, decide direction, translate, respond.
5) Persist audit record (optional) and update usage counters.

Error handling principles:
1) Fail fast for unsupported message types with a short guidance reply.
2) For transient translation failures: retry once with backoff; if still failing, respond with a human-readable error.
3) For language detection ambiguity: ask the user to choose direction or provide a one-tap setting command.

Group chat behavior (recommended defaults):
1) Only translate when the bot is directly replied to, or when a message starts with a trigger (for example, “tr:” or “translate ”). This prevents spam in groups.
2) Allow admins to enable always-on translation per group via a setting command.

Deployment shape:
1) One Node.js server process.
2) MongoDB for persistence.
3) Optional object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later add features like saving translation history exports or logs; not required for baseline translator.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal, production-friendly):

1) chats
Fields:
1) chatId (number/string)
2) type (private/group/supergroup)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) settings:
   1) mode: auto | locked
   2) lockedDirection: idtoen | entoid (only if mode=locked)
   3) groupTrigger: replyonly | prefixonly | always
   4) showDetectedLanguage: boolean
   5) showConfidenceWarnings: boolean
   6) maxCharsPerMessage: number

2) users
Fields:
1) userId
2) firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt
3) preferredMode (optional override for private chats)
4) usage:
   1) dayKey (YYYY-MM-DD)
   2) translatedCharsToday
   3) requestsToday

3) translationlogs (optional, can be disabled for privacy)
Purpose: debugging and quality improvements, not required for core functionality.
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) chatId, userId
3) inputLength
4) detectedSourceLang (id/en/unknown)
5) targetLang
6) confidence
7) providerLatencyMs
8) status (success/error)
9) errorCode (if any)

Privacy-first approach:
1) Do not store raw message text by default.
2) If logs are enabled, store only minimal metadata unless the user/admin explicitly opts in to diagnostics.
3) Implement retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.

Indexes:
1) chats.chatId unique
2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces the bot and starts auto-translation in the current chat. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows usage instructions, examples, and common troubleshooting. (usage: /help) [core]
• /mode — Sets translation mode: auto (detect) or locked (fixed direction). (usage: /mode auto | /mode locked) [settings]
• /id2en — Locks translation direction to Indonesian -> English for this chat. (usage: /id2en) [settings]
• /en2id — Locks translation direction to English -> Indonesian for this chat. (usage: /en2id) [settings]
• /auto — Shortcut to return to automatic language detection mode. (usage: /auto) [settings]
• /settings — Displays current chat settings and how to change them. (usage: /settings) [settings]
• /groupmode — Configures how the bot behaves in groups (admins only). (usage: /groupmode replyonly | /groupmode prefixonly | /groupmode always) [admin]
• /privacy — Explains what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /delete — Deletes stored user data for the requesting user (and private chat settings). (usage: /delete) [trust]
• /optoutlogs — Disables optional diagnostics logging for the current chat/user. (usage: /optoutlogs) [trust]
• /status — Shows basic service status and current rate limit counters for the user (optional). (usage: /status) [ops]

2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot automatically translates messages between Indonesian (id) and English (en).

Default behavior
In a new chat, auto-translate is ON and direction is AUTO.
That means:
1) Indonesian input is translated to English.
2) English input is translated to Indonesian.
3) If detection is uncertain, it defaults to translating into English.

Private vs group chats
1) Private chats: the bot replies to every non-command text message (unless auto-translate is turned off).
2) Group chats: the bot replies only when you reply to a bot message OR mention the bot.

Commands
/start
What it does: Welcome message and quick examples.
Usage: /start

/help
What it does: Detailed usage and limitations.
Usage: /help

/auto_on
What it does: Enables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_on

/auto_off
What it does: Disables auto-translate for the current chat.
Usage: /auto_off

/toen
What it does: Forces translation target to English for the current chat.
Usage: /toen

/toid
What it does: Forces translation target to Indonesian for the current chat.
Usage: /toid

/mode
What it does: Shows current settings for the chat.
Usage: /mode

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram bot token.

COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
Required. Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway.
Example format: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY
Required. API key for CookMyBots AI Gateway.

MONGODB_URI
Optional but recommended. MongoDB connection string used to persist per-chat settings.

LOG_LEVEL
Optional. Default is info.

Running locally
1) npm install
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and fill values
3) npm run dev

README.md.content:
This is a Telegram translation bot built with grammY (Node.js ES modules).

It automatically translates between Indonesian (id) and English (en).
If you send Indonesian, it replies in English. If you send English, it replies in Indonesian.

In group chats, the bot replies only when:
1) You reply to a bot message, or
2) You mention the bot (for example @YourBotUsername)

Setup
1) Install deps
npm install

2) Create a .env file (or set Render env vars) based on .env.sample

Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY

Optional but recommended
1) MONGODB_URI (persists per-chat settings across restarts)

Run locally
npm run dev

Run in production
npm start

Commands
/start
Shows a welcome message and quick examples.

/help
Shows detailed help.

/auto_on
Enable auto-translate for the current chat.

/auto_off
Disable auto-translate for the current chat.

/toen
Force translations to English for this chat.

/toid
Force translations to Indonesian for this chat.

/mode
Show current chat settings.

Notes
1) Only text messages are translated.
2) If MONGODB_URI is missing, the bot still works but settings reset on restart.
3) The bot uses CookMyBots AI Gateway only (no direct OpenAI calls).

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Indonesia–English Auto-Translate Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that automatically translates every incoming user message between Indonesian and English, reversing the translation direction based on detected input language. The bot works in private chats and can optionally support group chats with configurable behavior.

The plan focuses on a single-process Node.js service using grammY, MongoDB for persistence, and a modular architecture that supports rate limiting, abuse prevention, observability, and safe fallbacks when language detection is uncertain. The bot is designed to be simple for users (send text, get translation) while still being reliable and maintainable in production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Provide instant bidirectional translation between Indonesian and English. If a user sends Indonesian, the bot replies in English; if a user sends English, the bot replies in Indonesian.

User experience goals:
1) Zero-configuration default: Users can start translating immediately after /start.
2) Predictable replies: The bot responds to every supported message with a single translated response.
3) Fast and reliable: Low latency, with sensible fallbacks if detection or translation fails.
4) Clear behavior in edge cases: If language detection is ambiguous or text contains mixed languages, the bot explains what it did and offers a way to override.

Scope decisions:
1) Translate text content. Optionally include captions on photos/documents if present.
2) Do not attempt full OCR or speech-to-text as a baseline requirement.
3) Default translation direction is automatic, but users can lock direction (ID->EN or EN->ID) per chat.

Success criteria:
1) High accuracy for typical conversational text.
2) Minimal operational burden: stable single service, straightforward monitoring, and controlled costs.
3) Safe handling of spam and large messages while staying within Telegram limits.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) using grammY. The bot receives updates via either webhook or long polling. For production readiness, webhook is preferred if the environment supports inbound HTTP, otherwise long polling remains acceptable with careful error handling.

Core modules (separation of concerns):
1) Update handling layer: grammY bot instance, middleware chain, command router, message handlers.
2) Session and settings module: per-chat settings (auto vs locked direction, group behavior, verbosity), with MongoDB persistence.
3) Language detection module: lightweight detection tuned for Indonesian vs English, using a deterministic heuristic first and an external detector second (optional) to reduce cost/latency. Must return confidence score and a reason.
4) Translation provider module: abstraction over a translation API/service with retries, timeouts, circuit breaker behavior, and usage accounting.
5) Policy and safety module: rate limiting per user/chat, message size limits, content filtering for unsupported inputs, and group privacy controls.
6) Observability module: structured logging, metrics counters (translations, errors, latency), and correlation IDs per update.

Request lifecycle:
1) Receive update.
2) Identify chat/user, load settings and minimal user profile.
3) If command: execute command.
4) If text/caption: run policy checks, detect language, decide direction, translate, respond.
5) Persist audit record (optional) and update usage counters.

Error handling principles:
1) Fail fast for unsupported message types with a short guidance reply.
2) For transient translation failures: retry once with backoff; if still failing, respond with a human-readable error.
3) For language detection ambiguity: ask the user to choose direction or provide a one-tap setting command.

Group chat behavior (recommended defaults):
1) Only translate when the bot is directly replied to, or when a message starts with a trigger (for example, “tr:” or “translate ”). This prevents spam in groups.
2) Allow admins to enable always-on translation per group via a setting command.

Deployment shape:
1) One Node.js server process.
2) MongoDB for persistence.
3) Optional object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later add features like saving translation history exports or logs; not required for baseline translator.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal, production-friendly):

1) chats
Fields:
1) chatId (number/string)
2) type (private/group/supergroup)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) settings:
   1) mode: auto | locked
   2) lockedDirection: idtoen | entoid (only if mode=locked)
   3) groupTrigger: replyonly | prefixonly | always
   4) showDetectedLanguage: boolean
   5) showConfidenceWarnings: boolean
   6) maxCharsPerMessage: number

2) users
Fields:
1) userId
2) firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt
3) preferredMode (optional override for private chats)
4) usage:
   1) dayKey (YYYY-MM-DD)
   2) translatedCharsToday
   3) requestsToday

3) translationlogs (optional, can be disabled for privacy)
Purpose: debugging and quality improvements, not required for core functionality.
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) chatId, userId
3) inputLength
4) detectedSourceLang (id/en/unknown)
5) targetLang
6) confidence
7) providerLatencyMs
8) status (success/error)
9) errorCode (if any)

Privacy-first approach:
1) Do not store raw message text by default.
2) If logs are enabled, store only minimal metadata unless the user/admin explicitly opts in to diagnostics.
3) Implement retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.

Indexes:
1) chats.chatId unique
2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces the bot and starts auto-translation in the current chat. (usage: /start) [core]
• /help — Shows usage instructions, examples, and common troubleshooting. (usage: /help) [core]
• /mode — Sets translation mode: auto (detect) or locked (fixed direction). (usage: /mode auto | /mode locked) [settings]
• /id2en — Locks translation direction to Indonesian -> English for this chat. (usage: /id2en) [settings]
• /en2id — Locks translation direction to English -> Indonesian for this chat. (usage: /en2id) [settings]
• /auto — Shortcut to return to automatic language detection mode. (usage: /auto) [settings]
• /settings — Displays current chat settings and how to change them. (usage: /settings) [settings]
• /groupmode — Configures how the bot behaves in groups (admins only). (usage: /groupmode replyonly | /groupmode prefixonly | /groupmode always) [admin]
• /privacy — Explains what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [trust]
• /delete — Deletes stored user data for the requesting user (and private chat settings). (usage: /delete) [trust]
• /optoutlogs — Disables optional diagnostics logging for the current chat/user. (usage: /optoutlogs) [trust]
• /status — Shows basic service status and current rate limit counters for the user (optional). (usage: /status) [ops]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary goal: Provide instant bidirectional translation between Indonesian and English. If a user sends Indonesian, the bot replies in English; if a user sends English, the bot replies in Indonesian.

User experience goals:
1) Zero-configuration default: Users can start translating immediately after /start.
2) Predictable replies: The bot responds to every supported message with a single translated response.
3) Fast and reliable: Low latency, with sensible fallbacks if detection or translation fails.
4) Clear behavior in edge cases: If language detection is ambiguous or text contains mixed languages, the bot explains what it did and offers a way to override.

Scope decisions:
1) Translate text content. Optionally include captions on photos/documents if present.
2) Do not attempt full OCR or speech-to-text as a baseline requirement.
3) Default translation direction is automatic, but users can lock direction (ID->EN or EN->ID) per chat.

Success criteria:
1) High accuracy for typical conversational text.
2) Minimal operational burden: stable single service, straightforward monitoring, and controlled costs.
3) Safe handling of spam and large messages while staying within Telegram limits.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) using grammY. The bot receives updates via either webhook or long polling. For production readiness, webhook is preferred if the environment supports inbound HTTP, otherwise long polling remains acceptable with careful error handling.

Core modules (separation of concerns):
1) Update handling layer: grammY bot instance, middleware chain, command router, message handlers.
2) Session and settings module: per-chat settings (auto vs locked direction, group behavior, verbosity), with MongoDB persistence.
3) Language detection module: lightweight detection tuned for Indonesian vs English, using a deterministic heuristic first and an external detector second (optional) to reduce cost/latency. Must return confidence score and a reason.
4) Translation provider module: abstraction over a translation API/service with retries, timeouts, circuit breaker behavior, and usage accounting.
5) Policy and safety module: rate limiting per user/chat, message size limits, content filtering for unsupported inputs, and group privacy controls.
6) Observability module: structured logging, metrics counters (translations, errors, latency), and correlation IDs per update.

Request lifecycle:
1) Receive update.
2) Identify chat/user, load settings and minimal user profile.
3) If command: execute command.
4) If text/caption: run policy checks, detect language, decide direction, translate, respond.
5) Persist audit record (optional) and update usage counters.

Error handling principles:
1) Fail fast for unsupported message types with a short guidance reply.
2) For transient translation failures: retry once with backoff; if still failing, respond with a human-readable error.
3) For language detection ambiguity: ask the user to choose direction or provide a one-tap setting command.

Group chat behavior (recommended defaults):
1) Only translate when the bot is directly replied to, or when a message starts with a trigger (for example, “tr:” or “translate ”). This prevents spam in groups.
2) Allow admins to enable always-on translation per group via a setting command.

Deployment shape:
1) One Node.js server process.
2) MongoDB for persistence.
3) Optional object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later add features like saving translation history exports or logs; not required for baseline translator.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB collections (minimal, production-friendly):

1) chats
Fields:
1) chatId (number/string)
2) type (private/group/supergroup)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) settings:
   1) mode: auto | locked
   2) lockedDirection: idtoen | entoid (only if mode=locked)
   3) groupTrigger: replyonly | prefixonly | always
   4) showDetectedLanguage: boolean
   5) showConfidenceWarnings: boolean
   6) maxCharsPerMessage: number

2) users
Fields:
1) userId
2) firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt
3) preferredMode (optional override for private chats)
4) usage:
   1) dayKey (YYYY-MM-DD)
   2) translatedCharsToday
   3) requestsToday

3) translationlogs (optional, can be disabled for privacy)
Purpose: debugging and quality improvements, not required for core functionality.
Fields:
1) timestamp
2) chatId, userId
3) inputLength
4) detectedSourceLang (id/en/unknown)
5) targetLang
6) confidence
7) providerLatencyMs
8) status (success/error)
9) errorCode (if any)

Privacy-first approach:
1) Do not store raw message text by default.
2) If logs are enabled, store only minimal metadata unless the user/admin explicitly opts in to diagnostics.
3) Implement retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.

Indexes:
1) chats.chatId unique
2) users.userId unique
3) translationlogs.timestamp TTL index (if using retention)

In-memory maps:
1) Short-lived caches for chat settings and user usage counters to reduce DB reads, with periodic sync to MongoDB.
2) In-flight request map per chat/user to avoid overlapping translations when users spam messages quickly (optional).
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User sends /start.
2) Bot replies with a brief description: send any text in Indonesian or English to translate.
3) Bot shows current mode (auto) and quick commands to lock direction.
4) Bot initializes user and chat records in MongoDB.

2) Auto translation flow (private chat)
1) User sends a text message.
2) Policy checks: length, rate limits.
3) Detect language: classify as Indonesian, English, or unknown with confidence.
4) Decide target language:
   1) If detected Indonesian: target English.
   2) If detected English: target Indonesian.
   3) If unknown or low confidence: either ask user to choose, or default to the last successful direction in this chat with a warning.
5) Translate via provider module with timeout and retry.
6) Reply with translated text.
7) Optionally append a small note when detection was uncertain (configurable).

3) Locked direction flow
1) User runs /mode locked or /id2en or /en2id.
2) Bot updates chat settings.
3) Subsequent messages translate according to locked direction without detection.
4) /mode auto returns to detection-based behavior.

4) Group chat flow
Default behavior: translate only when explicitly invoked.
1) If groupTrigger=replyonly:
   1) Bot checks if the message is a reply to a bot message or mentions the bot.
   2) If yes, translate; if no, ignore.
2) If groupTrigger=prefixonly:
   1) Bot checks for a prefix like “tr:” and strips it before translating.
3) If groupTrigger=always:
   1) Bot translates all messages (not recommended by default).
Admin commands configure this per group.

5) Rate limiting and abuse prevention
1) Per-user: cap requests per minute and characters per minute.
2) Per-chat: cap overall throughput in groups.
3) On limit hit: respond with a short message indicating cooldown.
4) Track usage in MongoDB with daily counters.

6) Edge cases
1) Very short messages (for example, “ok”, “ya”): treat as ambiguous. Use chat history direction heuristic or ask user for direction.
2) Mixed-language messages: choose dominant language by heuristic, or offer a quick override command.
3) Non-text messages: if caption exists, translate caption; otherwise reply with guidance.
4) Provider failure: return a friendly error, encourage retry, and do not spam retries.

7) Observability and operations flow
1) Each update gets a correlation ID.
2) Log key events: detection result, chosen direction, provider latency, error codes.
3) Periodic health checks: database connectivity, provider reachability.
4) Alerting is out of scope for this plan, but logs and metrics must be sufficient to add it later without architectural changes.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and language:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for chat/user settings, usage counters, and optional translation logs.
2) Small in-memory caches/maps for hot settings and rate-limiting windows, with MongoDB as source of truth.

External services:
1) Translation API/provider accessed over HTTPS, wrapped behind an internal provider abstraction to allow swapping providers without touching bot logic.
2) Optional lightweight language detection library locally; otherwise rely on provider detection if supported.

Storage:
1) No file storage required for baseline.
2) If later needed (exports, diagnostics attachments), use object storage or simple filesystem storage.

Deployment:
1) A Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram token loaded from environment variable TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
3) Configuration via environment variables (MongoDB connection string, provider keys, rate limit thresholds, logging level).

Operational best practices:
1) Graceful shutdown to finish in-flight translations and close MongoDB connections.
2) Timeouts and retries for all outbound HTTP calls.
3) Input validation and strict limits to prevent memory bloat and provider overuse.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability:
1) Use timeouts for translation requests and limit concurrency.
2) Implement a simple circuit breaker behavior: if provider failures spike, temporarily fail fast with a clear error to avoid cascading timeouts.
3) Ensure idempotent handling where possible; avoid duplicate replies by tracking last processed update IDs in memory and/or MongoDB if webhook retries are frequent.

Performance:
1) Keep detection lightweight; avoid calling the translation provider twice.
2) Cache chat settings and user limits briefly in memory.
3) Enforce message size limits and truncate safely with a note when needed.

Security:
1) Store secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate incoming updates (webhook secret path or similar technique) if using webhooks.
3) Avoid logging message text by default.
4) Enforce anti-spam limits and ignore obvious automation loops.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Provide /privacy to explain what is stored.
2) Offer /optoutlogs to disable optional diagnostics logging.
3) Provide /delete to remove user data (user record and chat settings if private chat).

Maintainability:
1) Clear module boundaries: Telegram handling vs detection vs translation vs persistence.
2) Provider abstraction for translation.
3) Centralized configuration and consistent error taxonomy.

Test strategy (high-level):
1) Unit tests for detection and direction decision.
2) Integration tests for translation provider wrapper with mocked HTTP.
3) End-to-end tests using Telegram update fixtures to validate grammY middleware routing.
